presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: I've had a cough for months that won't go away
Persistent cough, weight loss and night sweats
I've been soaking through my clothes at night and I'm losing weight
I've occasionally coughed up blood and I'm exhausted all the time
I've been feeling unwell for weeks — cough, sweating and no appetite

history_data.hpi: Patient presents with an 8-week history of persistent productive cough with occasional blood-streaked sputum. Associated symptoms include drenching night sweats requiring change of clothing, unintentional weight loss of approximately 7kg over 2 months, progressive fatigue, and markedly reduced appetite. Low-grade fever has been present most evenings. Patient emigrated from a TB-endemic country 3 years ago and works in a crowded warehouse with poor ventilation. No known TB contacts but lives in shared accommodation with 6 others. No previous TB diagnosis or treatment. Not known to be HIV positive but has not been recently tested.
patient_quotes: The cough has been there for months — it just won't go away.
I wake up drenched in sweat most nights — I have to change my clothes.
I've lost a lot of weight without trying — my clothes don't fit anymore.
Sometimes when I cough I see a little blood in the phlegm.
I feel exhausted all the time and I have no appetite.
pmh_pool: No previous TB diagnosis or treatment
Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed 4 years ago, on metformin (diabetes increases TB reactivation risk significantly)
No known HIV infection — not recently tested
Smoking history — current smoker, 10 cigarettes per day
Born and raised in high TB-burden country — emigrated 3 years ago

social_history.smoking: Current smoker — 10 cigarettes per day for 12 years (approximately 6 pack-years)
social_history.exercise: Reduced significantly due to fatigue and breathlessness
social_history.stress: Significant — financial pressures, immigration stressors, crowded living situation

negative_history: No previous TB diagnosis or treatment
No recent foreign travel beyond usual residence
No haemoptysis of large volume — no frank blood

general_appearance: Visibly cachectic — marked muscle wasting and weight loss
No acute respiratory distress at rest
respiratory: Reduced air entry — right upper lobe
Dullness to percussion — right upper zone
Mild bronchial breath sounds — right apex
lymph_nodes: Right cervical lymphadenopathy — single firm, non-tender node approximately 1cm

investigations.Chest X-Ray: Right upper lobe cavitary lesion with surrounding nodular infiltrates and fibrosis. Apical shadowing with cavitation. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy visible on plain film. Appearances highly suspicious for active pulmonary tuberculosis. CT chest recommended for further characterisation.
investigations.CT Chest: Right upper lobe cavitary lesion 3.2cm with surrounding satellite nodules and tree-in-bud pattern consistent with endobronchial spread. Bilateral upper lobe infiltrates. Right hilar lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No pericardial effusion. CT findings highly consistent with active pulmonary TB with endobronchial spread — indicates likely infectiousness.
investigations.Sputum AFB Smear: Sputum acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear positive — 3+ on Ziehl-Neelsen staining. Three early morning sputum samples collected. Positive smear confirms presence of mycobacteria; culture required for speciation and drug susceptibility testing (DST). Patient should be considered infectious until proven otherwise.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 10.4 × 10⁹/L (mildly elevated), Lymphocytes 0.9 × 10⁹/L (lymphopaenia — associated with TB and possible HIV co-infection), Hb 10.8 g/dL (normocytic anaemia — anaemia of chronic disease), MCV 84 fL, Platelets 468 × 10⁹/L (reactive thrombocytosis). Lymphopaenia warrants HIV testing.
investigations.ESR: 112 mm/hr — markedly elevated, consistent with active chronic infection and inflammation.
investigations.CRP: 88 mg/L — significantly elevated, consistent with active infection.
investigations.Blood Glucose: 9.4 mmol/L — elevated in known diabetic. Poor glycaemic control increases TB severity and treatment response. HbA1c required.
investigations.HbA1c: 74 mmol/mol (8.9%) — suboptimal glycaemic control. Diabetes is a major risk factor for TB reactivation and is associated with worse treatment outcomes. Intensification of diabetes management required alongside TB treatment.
investigations.LFTs (Liver Function Tests): ALT 36 IU/L, ALP 88 IU/L, Bilirubin 10 µmol/L, Albumin 28 g/L (hypoalbuminaemia — malnutrition and chronic disease). Baseline LFTs critical before starting anti-TB therapy — all four first-line drugs (HRZE) are potentially hepatotoxic. Monitor closely during treatment.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 135 mmol/L, K 3.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 84 µmol/L, Urea 5.6 mmol/L — normal. Baseline renal function required before starting ethambutol (renally cleared — dose adjustment needed in renal impairment).

hidden_diagnosis: Active Pulmonary Tuberculosis — Smear Positive, Cavitary Disease

patient_rules.emotional_state: fatigued and anxious — has noticed symptoms worsening for weeks, concerned about what is wrong, worried about implications for family and housemates

essential_questions: duration of cough — weeks to months is a key TB red flag
character of cough — productive, blood-streaked sputum
night sweats — severity, drenching vs mild
unintentional weight loss — amount and timeframe
fatigue and anorexia — constitutional B symptoms
fever — pattern, low-grade evening fevers typical of TB
country of origin or birth — TB-endemic country
recent travel to high TB-burden regions
known TB contacts — household, workplace, community
living conditions — crowded housing, hostel, prison
HIV status — must be tested in all TB patients
previous TB diagnosis or treatment — risk of drug resistance
diabetes mellitus — major TB reactivation risk factor
occupation — healthcare worker, prison worker, teacher
essential_examinations: general assessment — cachexia, pallor, fever, chronically unwell
respiratory examination — upper lobe crackles, bronchial breathing, reduced air entry apically
percussion — dullness over consolidation or effusion
lymph node examination — cervical, supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
abdominal examination — hepatosplenomegaly in miliary TB
critical_actions: immediately place patient in airborne isolation — negative pressure room if available; surgical mask on patient during transport
notify infectious disease or respiratory TB team urgently
collect three early morning sputum samples for AFB smear and TB culture before starting treatment
order chest X-ray — upper lobe cavitation, infiltrates, apical fibrosis are classic
arrange CT chest for further characterisation of extent of disease
HIV test — mandatory in all TB patients; HIV co-infection fundamentally changes management
baseline LFTs before starting anti-TB therapy — all four drugs are hepatotoxic
baseline renal function — ethambutol dose adjustment required in renal impairment
baseline visual acuity and colour vision — ethambutol can cause optic neuritis
initiate standard 4-drug anti-TB therapy: HRZE for 2 months (intensive phase) then HR for 4 months (continuation phase) — once drug susceptibility confirmed
await drug susceptibility testing results — multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) requires specialist management
contact tracing — all household contacts and close contacts must be screened
mandatory public health notification — TB is a notifiable disease
optimise diabetes control — poor glycaemic control worsens TB outcomes
directly observed therapy (DOT) should be considered to ensure adherence
red_flags: Haemoptysis with chronic cough — TB until proven otherwise
Drenching night sweats + weight loss + chronic cough — classic TB triad
Upper lobe cavitation on CXR — highly characteristic of post-primary TB
AFB smear positive — infectious, immediate isolation mandatory
Lymphopaenia on FBC — raises concern for HIV co-infection
Diabetes with chronic respiratory symptoms — TB reactivation risk
Origin from or travel to high TB-burden country
Crowded living conditions — high transmission risk
Previous TB treatment — risk of drug-resistant TB
Known HIV positive status — risk of atypical or disseminated TB
acceptable_differentials: Primary lung cancer — chronic cough, haemoptysis, weight loss, upper lobe mass; biopsy distinguishes; TB and cancer can coexist
Community-acquired pneumonia — acute onset, fever, productive cough; CXR consolidation; but lacks chronicity and night sweats
Lung abscess — cavitary lesion, foul sputum, acute illness; anaerobic organisms; no weight loss or night sweats pattern
Bronchiectasis — chronic productive cough, recurrent infections; CXR shows dilated airways not upper lobe cavitation
Fungal lung infection (aspergillosis, histoplasmosis) — immunocompromised patients; travel history; serology and culture distinguish
Lymphoma — B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), mediastinal lymphadenopathy; biopsy required
management_actions: Airborne isolation immediately — negative pressure room, N95 mask for healthcare workers, surgical mask on patient
Three early morning sputum samples — AFB smear, TB culture, and drug susceptibility testing (DST)
Chest X-Ray and CT Chest — characterise extent of disease
Baseline bloods: FBC, LFTs, U&E, blood glucose, HbA1c, ESR, CRP
Visual acuity and colour vision assessment — baseline before ethambutol
Standard anti-TB therapy: Isoniazid (H) + Rifampicin (R) + Pyrazinamide (Z) + Ethambutol (E) for 2 months, then Isoniazid + Rifampicin for 4 months
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation with isoniazid — prevents peripheral neuropathy
Await DST results — modify regimen if MDR-TB confirmed
Monitor LFTs monthly during treatment — hepatotoxicity risk with HRZE
Monthly visual acuity monitoring — ethambutol optic neuritis
Optimise glycaemic control — increase metformin or add second agent
Contact tracing — all household contacts and close contacts
Consider directly observed therapy (DOT) for adherence assurance
critical_history_questions: How long have you had this cough — has it changed over time?
Have you coughed up any blood — how much and how often?
Do you wake up sweating at night — do you have to change your clothes or bedding?
How much weight have you lost and over what period of time?
Have you had a fever — does it tend to come in the evenings?
Where were you born and where have you lived?
Have you spent time in any country with a high rate of TB?
Has anyone close to you had tuberculosis — at home, at work, or in your community?
How many people do you live with and in how many rooms?
Have you ever been tested or treated for TB before?
Have you ever been tested for HIV — would you be willing to have a test today?
Do you have diabetes or any condition affecting your immune system?
Are you on any steroids or other medications that affect your immune system?
Do you work in healthcare, a prison, or another high-risk environment?
Have you had a BCG vaccination?
